questions.question: Why does Meta's feed ranking use a three-stage funnel (retrieval → lightweight scoring → heavy ranking) instead of a single model that scores all candidates?
options: A single heavy model scoring 10,000 candidates would require ~10,000 × 0.1ms = 1 second of model inference time, far exceeding the 200ms end-to-end budget. The funnel eliminates 99.5% of candidates cheaply, so the expensive model only runs on the top 500.
The three-stage funnel is required by EU DSA regulations to ensure fair candidate selection before ML ranking.
A single model cannot process more than 500 items at once due to GPU memory constraints.
The three-stage funnel is used to ensure that posts from paid advertisers are evaluated separately from organic content.
questions.explanation: Latency arithmetic drives the multi-stage design. If the heavy model takes 0.04ms per item and you have 10,000 candidates, that is 400ms just for inference — the entire budget blown before any network latency.

questions.question: What is the difference between online features and offline features in a feature store?
options: Online features (stored in Redis/Memcached) are served in milliseconds at request time and include real-time signals like recent interactions. Offline features (in Hive/batch store) are computed over large datasets and updated daily, covering long-term patterns.
Online features are features from users who are currently active; offline features are from users who haven't logged in recently.
Online features require a GPU to compute; offline features can be computed on CPU.
Online features are user features; offline features are item features.
questions.explanation: The split is about latency and freshness. Online features must be retrieved in <5ms at ranking time, so they live in Redis. Offline features are computed by large Spark jobs that run daily and are too expensive to compute in real time.

questions.question: A new user signs up and opens their feed for the first time. They have no engagement history, no follow graph, and no interest profile. What is the correct cold-start strategy?
options: Fall back to interest graph signals from onboarding (if user selected interests), trending content in their region, and content from demographically similar new users. After 3–5 interactions, short-term features update in minutes and personalization kicks in quickly.
Show the user only content from Meta's official accounts until they have followed at least 10 people.
Disable ranking entirely for new users and show content in reverse chronological order until 30 days of history accumulate.
Assign the new user a random existing user's feature vector as a proxy until real history develops.
questions.explanation: Cold start is a sequence: onboarding signals → regional trending → fast feedback from first interactions. The key is that Flink updates short-term features within minutes, so even the first session's interactions rapidly improve the next ranking.

questions.question: The heavy ranking model's p99 inference latency suddenly spikes from 18ms to 80ms during a model deployment. What is the correct system response?
options: The circuit breaker trips: ranking falls back to the lightweight scorer's output as the final ranking result. Users see a slightly less accurate feed but the pipeline still returns within the 200ms SLA.
The system waits for the latency to recover, queuing ranking requests until the GPU cluster stabilizes.
Ranking is disabled entirely and feeds are served in reverse chronological order until the GPU cluster recovers.
The latency spike is logged but ignored because 80ms is still within the total 200ms budget.
questions.explanation: Graceful degradation through the circuit breaker pattern is the correct answer. The lightweight scorer result is already a reasonable ranking — it's worse than the heavy model but far better than chronological. Maintaining SLA matters more than perfect ranking accuracy.

questions.question: After deploying a new ranking model, you observe that the recommendation diversity metric dropped 30% — users are seeing mostly the same creator repeatedly. What caused this and how do you fix it?
options: The new model overfit to engagement signals during training, creating a positive feedback loop where already-popular creators get ranked higher, driving more engagement, which further reinforces their ranking. Fix: rollback the model, increase exploration injection rate, and add a diversity constraint in the post-ranking filter.
The diversity metric dropped because the new model is more accurate and users naturally prefer content from fewer creators.
The Redis feature store has stale creator score features that are inflating scores for a small set of creators.
The FAISS ANN index was not rebuilt after the model update, causing it to return candidates from the old embedding space.
questions.explanation: This is the feedback loop / filter bubble problem. The model optimizes for engagement, and engagement data is dominated by already-popular content. Without diversity constraints and exploration injection, the model amplifies homogeneity over time.

questions.question: Why does Meta use FAISS for candidate retrieval instead of a standard database query?
options: FAISS performs approximate nearest neighbor search over high-dimensional embeddings (128-dim interest vectors) across 1B+ posts in under 5ms. A standard SQL query cannot perform vector similarity search at this scale and latency.
FAISS is faster than SQL because it uses flash storage instead of disk-based storage.
FAISS is required because post embeddings cannot be represented as SQL columns.
FAISS eliminates the need for a follow graph because it ranks all posts globally by interest similarity.
questions.explanation: Vector similarity search is fundamentally different from key-value or range lookup. FAISS uses inverted file indexes with product quantization to approximate nearest neighbors in high-dimensional spaces — no SQL database does this efficiently at 1B items.

questions.question: The EU Digital Services Act requires users to understand why a specific post appeared at the top of their feed. What does your design need to support this?
options: Log the top-3 feature contributions (SHAP-style) for each ranking decision, store them in an audit log, and expose a user-facing explanation endpoint. Explanations are generated from the feature attribution data: 'Shown because you recently engaged with [creator] and this post has high engagement in your network.'
The model must be a simple decision tree so its reasoning is inherently interpretable.
Providing post-hoc explanations is sufficient — no changes to the ranking pipeline are needed, just a legal disclaimer.
Users must opt in to personalized ranking to comply with DSA; all opt-out users receive reverse chronological feed.
questions.explanation: DSA Article 27 requires recommender systems to disclose the 'main parameters' used in recommendations. SHAP-style attribution logged per decision enables both user-facing explanations and regulatory audits without changing the model architecture.

questions.question: You want to A/B test two new ranking models (model A and model B) alongside the production model, each on 5% of traffic, without the experiment groups contaminating each other. What is the correct design?
options: Use deterministic hashing of user_id to assign each user to exactly one bucket (control, model-A, model-B). Each user always gets the same model for the duration of the experiment. Enforce mutual exclusion: no user can be in two experiments that affect the same feed surface simultaneously.
Randomly assign each feed request to a model — the same user may get model A for one session and model B for the next, which is acceptable because the effects average out.
Run experiments sequentially: deploy model A for one week, then model B the next week, then compare metrics.
Route traffic by geography: model A for US users, model B for EU users, production for everyone else.
questions.explanation: User-level deterministic bucketing prevents the same user from seeing different models, which would pollute both experiment cohorts. Mutual exclusion prevents a user from being in overlapping experiments that would make attribution ambiguous. Time-based and geographic splits introduce confounds.
